answer.content:
Support Vector Machine Interactive Explorer

This demonstration lets you:

  • Generate and manipulate datasets (2D points) from various classes.
  • Train SVM classifiers with different kernels, parameters, and hyperparameter tuning.
  • Visualize the data points, decision boundary, margins, and support vectors.
  • Manually add, move, or remove points to explore the effects on the SVM.
  • Examine hinge loss and margins per point.
  • Perform batch training, save/load datasets and models in JSON.
  • Explore multi-class classification via one-vs-one or one-vs-rest approaches.
  • Compare custom linear solver and simplified SMO solver implementations.

Dataset Management


 

Click on canvas to add points with selected class.

SVM Training Parameters










Visualization & Interaction

Use mouse interaction in canvas:

  • Left Click: Add points (if Add Point Mode ON) or Select nearest point.
  • Drag Selected Point: Move its position and retrain SVM automatically.
  • Delete Selected Point: Press Del key to remove selected point and retrain.
  • Mouse Wheel: Zoom in/out centered on mouse pointer.
  • Right Click + Drag: Pan canvas.

State Info, Losses, and Model Export/Import

Selected Point Info: (None)
Current Model & Dataset JSON:

Hyperparameter Grid Search / Cross-Validation





Grid Search Results:







Multi-Class Settings






Note: Multi-class only available for linear kernel and simplified SMO solver (for demo).

messages.content:
Generate a single, self-contained HTML file with embedded CSS and JavaScript that demonstrates the concept Support Vector Machine with MAXIMUM interactivity and rich user control. Focus on:

- Multiple interactive controls (buttons, sliders, input fields)
- Complex state transitions and user-driven workflows
- Deep exploration capabilities with many possible interaction paths
- Rich functionality as the PRIMARY goal

IMPORTANT: Keep visual design EXTREMELY MINIMAL and BASIC - use simple black text on white background, basic HTML elements with no styling, no colors, no animations. The interface should be functional but visually plain and unpolished. Prioritize interactive richness over visual appeal.

Only respond with the complete HTML file.
